card.indications: Used with other antiretroviral medications to treat HIV-1 infection. It is not used alone.
card.mechanism: Abacavir is converted inside cells to an active carbovir triphosphate metabolite. It competes with the natural building blocks used by HIV reverse transcriptase, causing viral DNA chain termination and reducing HIV replication.
card.dosing: Adults: 600 mg/day by mouth as 300 mg twice daily or 600 mg once daily. Pediatric patients age 3 months and older: weight-based dosing, maximum 600 mg/day. Mild hepatic impairment: 200 mg twice daily using oral solution. Moderate or severe hepatic impairment: contraindicated.
card.routeAdministration: Oral only. Tablets and oral solution may be taken with or without food. Confirm the patient can swallow the tablet; use oral solution when a liquid formulation is needed. Give as part of a complete antiretroviral regimen.
card.contraindications: Positive HLA-B*5701 test; previous hypersensitivity reaction to abacavir; moderate or severe hepatic impairment.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for serious and sometimes fatal multi-organ hypersensitivity. Screen for HLA-B*5701 before starting or restarting. Stop immediately when hypersensitivity is suspected and never restart if it cannot be ruled out. Also monitor for lactic acidosis, severe hepatomegaly with steatosis, immune reconstitution syndrome, and possible cardiovascular risk.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headache, fatigue or malaise, sleep disturbance or abnormal dreams, rash, abdominal discomfort, and dizziness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fatal hypersensitivity or anaphylaxis; lactic acidosis; severe hepatotoxicity or fatty liver; immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome; Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis; hypotension, respiratory failure, renal failure, or liver failure associated with hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: Methadone exposure may decrease, so some patients may need a methadone dose adjustment. Riociguat exposure may increase and may require dose reduction. Review the complete antiretroviral regimen for duplicate abacavir-containing products.
card.monitoring: Before therapy: HLA-B*5701, medication and allergy history, liver function, HIV viral load, and CD4 count. During therapy: assess for hypersensitivity symptoms, adherence, viral load and CD4 response, liver enzymes, signs of lactic acidosis, and symptoms of immune reconstitution. Consider cardiovascular risk factors.
card.patientTeaching: Carry and read the abacavir Warning Card. Report fever, rash, nausea/vomiting/diarrhea or abdominal pain, severe fatigue or achiness, shortness of breath, cough, or sore throat immediately—especially when symptoms occur in two or more groups. Never restart after a suspected hypersensitivity reaction unless specifically directed after medical evaluation. Take exactly as prescribed, do not double missed doses, and do not run out.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Food does not meaningfully change exposure. Maintain hydration if nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea occurs. Report poor intake, persistent GI symptoms, unusual weakness, or abdominal swelling because these may signal serious toxicity.

dosageForms: Scored tablets: 1 mg, 2 mg, 2.5 mg, 3 mg, 4 mg, 5 mg, 6 mg, 7.5 mg, and 10 mg

card.indications: Prevention and treatment of venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism; prevention of thromboembolic complications with atrial fibrillation or cardiac valve replacement; and reduction of recurrent myocardial infarction, stroke, or systemic embolism risk after myocardial infarction.
card.mechanism: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase, reducing regeneration of active vitamin K. This lowers hepatic production of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and the anticoagulant proteins C and S.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized according to the patient’s INR response. A common initial dose is 2 to 5 mg by mouth once daily, followed by adjustment to the ordered therapeutic INR. Lower starting doses may be needed for older adults, debilitated patients, patients with liver disease, or those at high bleeding risk. Never adjust from a fixed dose without INR-guided prescriber instructions.
card.routeAdministration: Oral only. Give at the same time each day, with or without food. Verify the tablet strength carefully because different strengths are color-coded. Maintain consistent product and dosing schedule when possible.
card.contraindications: Pregnancy except in selected patients with mechanical heart valves when benefits outweigh risks; active major bleeding; recent or planned high-risk surgery involving the central nervous system or eye; severe uncontrolled hypertension; and conditions in which reliable INR monitoring or safe administration cannot be maintained.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for major or fatal bleeding. Risk rises with high INR, older age, history of bleeding, hypertension, cerebrovascular disease, anemia, cancer, trauma, interacting drugs, or inconsistent diet. Other serious risks include tissue necrosis, calciphylaxis, cholesterol microembolization, acute kidney injury, and fetal harm.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Minor bruising, nosebleeds, bleeding gums, prolonged bleeding from cuts, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, rash, and hair loss.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Major or fatal hemorrhage; intracranial or gastrointestinal bleeding; skin or tissue necrosis; calciphylaxis; purple toe syndrome or cholesterol emboli; acute kidney injury associated with excessive anticoagulation; and fetal injury or death.
card.interactions: Many prescription drugs, over-the-counter medicines, antibiotics, antifungals, NSAIDs, antiplatelet drugs, SSRIs/SNRIs, amiodarone, alcohol, herbal products, and supplements can raise or lower INR or bleeding risk. Check with the prescriber or pharmacist before starting, stopping, or changing any medication or supplement.
card.monitoring: Check baseline PT/INR, CBC, hemoglobin/hematocrit, platelet count, liver function, renal function, medication list, diet pattern, and bleeding history. Monitor INR frequently during initiation and after dose, diet, illness, or medication changes; once stable, testing is commonly every 1 to 4 weeks. Assess for occult or visible bleeding, bruising, falls, neurologic changes, and signs of thrombosis.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed at the same time daily. Do not double a missed dose; follow the anticoagulation clinic’s instructions. Keep all INR appointments. Report unusual bruising, prolonged bleeding, black or bloody stools, red or brown urine, vomiting or coughing blood, severe headache, dizziness, weakness, a fall, or any head injury immediately. Use a soft toothbrush and electric razor, avoid high-injury activities, wear medical-alert identification, and tell every healthcare provider and dentist that you take warfarin.
card.nutrition: Keep vitamin K intake consistent from week to week rather than avoiding it completely. Large sudden changes in leafy green vegetables, vegetable oils, supplements, nutrition shakes, or herbal products can alter INR. Avoid binge drinking and discuss alcohol use with the prescriber. Ask before using cranberry, grapefruit, herbal teas, or supplements because some products may affect anticoagulation.
